groups.name: Yellowstone universe
groups.explanation: Rooster (a character in Yellowstone), John Dutton (the central character played by Kevin Costner), Yellowstone (the show title itself), and Kevin Costner (lead actor) — however, solvers must notice that Kevin Costner and John Dutton are both on the board, which creates a deliberate near-redundancy trap. They belong together in the same group, but seeing both tiles may make solvers second-guess whether one belongs elsewhere.

groups.name: TV comedies that ended deliberately on the creators' own terms
groups.explanation: Ted Lasso (ended after Season 3 as the creators planned), Schitt's Creek (ended after Season 6 entirely on Dan and Eugene Levy's terms), Detectorists (Mackenzie Crook chose to end it after three series), and Derry Girls (Lisa McGee deliberately concluded it after three series) — all beloved comedy series that ended exactly when and how their creators intended, not due to cancellation.

groups.name: Morally compromised TV characters widely acknowledged as the most capable person in their world
groups.explanation: Omar Little (The Wire — principled outlaw but the most feared and capable operator in Baltimore), Vic Mackey (The Shield — deeply corrupt cop but undeniably the most effective officer in Farmington), Jimmy McNulty (The Wire — self-destructive and insubordinate but the sharpest detective on the force), and Al Swearengen (Deadwood — murderous saloon owner who is nonetheless the most effective political mind in camp) are all deeply flawed characters their shows acknowledge as the most capable in their environment.

groups.name: Female TV characters disproportionately blamed by audiences more than the men around them
groups.explanation: Carmela Soprano (The Sopranos), Skyler White (Breaking Bad), Betty Draper (Mad Men), and Peggy Olson (Mad Men) — all female characters who attracted intense, disproportionate audience hostility compared to the objectively worse men around them, a documented cultural phenomenon in prestige TV criticism. The trap is that solvers expect 'wives of antiheroes,' but Peggy Olson is not a wife of an antihero — she is a colleague and peer of Don Draper who nonetheless drew audience resentment for her ambition, marking this as a misogyny-in-fandom group rather than a simple 'antihero wives' group. Joan Holloway was removed from the original design because her case, while valid culturally, is less immediately legible as fitting a crisp category alongside the others.
